### High-Severity Findings (4)

**STRUCTURAL-001 — Double ToolSurfaceTitle render** (HIGH, both reviewers)
`SessionZeroSeedCanvas.kt` calls `ToolSurface(surfaceTitle = "Session Zero · Seed", ...)` AND then calls `ToolSurfaceTitle(text = "Session Zero · Seed")` explicitly inside the Column content block at line 89. The `ToolSurface` component already renders the title from its `surfaceTitle` parameter — the second call produces a duplicate title in the UI.

**ACCURACY-001 — Readback format missing "level " prefix** (HIGH, both reviewers)
The AC3 canonical format is `"{partySize} at the table, level {level}, {shapeOfStart}"` — "level" is a static prefix outside the variable. The implementation produces `"$partySize at the table, $startingLevel, $shapeOfStart"` — omitting the word "level". The readback test masks this by setting `startingLevel = "level one (fresh at the start)"` (the word "level" embedded in the value string), so the test passes without catching the missing prefix. A plain `startingLevel = "5"` would output "4 at the table, 5, setting out tomorrow" instead of "4 at the table, level 5, setting out tomorrow."

**ACCURACY-002 — ARCH-VM2 persistence format deviation** (HIGH, both reviewers)
Architecture ARCH-VM2 specifies `currentPhase: Int` (phase index 1–5) as the DataStore persistence format. The implementation persists `phase.name` (String: "PHASE_1_IDENTITY", etc.). The internal round-trip works, but any external component expecting an integer phase index receives unexpected data and the architecture contract is violated.

**COHERENCE-001 — Invented out-of-spec Urd voice lines in screen** (HIGH, both reviewers)
`CampaignInitScreen.kt` contains three sets of raw string literals for Urd voice lines not specified in any AC: (1) a system acknowledgment when the Spinner selects a game system (lines 233–245), (2) a "tell me the number" Urd prompt (lines 253–258), and (3) a PHASE_1 opening greeting (lines 446–451). The story's DoD rule explicitly prohibits raw string literals for Urd's voice lines in the screen — they must be constants in the ViewModel companion object.

---

### Medium-Severity Findings (5)

**STRUCTURAL-002 — Missing platform ChatViewModel files** (MEDIUM)
The story's File List documents `desktopApp/ChatViewModel.kt` and `composeApp/ChatViewModel.kt` as modified to add `addNornMessage()`. Neither is present in the outputs. The `ChatActions` interface has no implementing class in the output set, leaving the integration bridge incomplete.

**STRUCTURAL-003 — NornRadii.card unverified cross-reference** (MEDIUM)
`CampaignCard.kt` imports and uses `NornRadii.card`. The story Dev Notes explicitly say the object "does not yet exist" and instruct using `RoundedCornerShape(12.dp)` with a comment. The implementation's comment claims "NornRadii object now exists" — unsupported by any evidence in the outputs. If `NornRadii` is absent from the project, this file fails to compile.

**ACCURACY-003 — URD_CLOSING_LINE visibility spec inconsistency** (MEDIUM)
The story Dev Notes specify `private const val URD_CLOSING_LINE` but the screen needs to reference it as `CampaignInitViewModel.URD_CLOSING_LINE`, requiring it to be public. The implementation correctly made it public, but the spec inconsistency is unacknowledged.

**COHERENCE-002 — Redundant `_heroesRemainingValue` field** (MEDIUM)
`_heroesRemainingValue` is written in `commitPartySize` but never read after the StateFlow is initialized. Two writeable references to the same logical state reduce clarity and create a maintenance hazard.

**COHERENCE-003 — `clearJourneyState` name vs. sentinel-write behavior** (MEDIUM)
`clearJourneyState()` removes `KEY_CURRENT_PHASE` then writes `PHASE_5_CLOSE` back to it. The function name and KDoc say "clears" but the function also writes a new value. The dual responsibility is not reflected in the name.

---

### Low-Severity Findings (4)

**STRUCTURAL-004** — `_heroesRemainingValue` is dead code after initialization (low)
**STRUCTURAL-005** — `surfaceTitle` / `ToolSurfaceTitle` uses mixed case "Session Zero · Seed" where AC5 specifies all-caps "SESSION ZERO · SEED" (low)
**ACCURACY-004** — "not now" added to NEGATIVE_PHRASES without spec backing in AC4 or Dev Notes (low)
**COHERENCE-004** — "tell-me-the-number detection" test is tautological — asserts hardcoded string literals, calls no ViewModel method (low)

---

### What Passed

The implementation gets the core logic right across the board: CampaignInitPhase enum with all 5 values matching AC1 exactly; hero loop countdown and StateFlow emissions; Oxford-comma list builder; sentiment parser ordering (negatives before positives to prevent "not tonight" → "tonight" false match, with `\bno\b` word boundary); `journeyComplete` SharedFlow; AppPrefs persistence with corruption recovery; all AC11 test cases present using Kotest FunSpec + Turbine; no MockK in commonTest; no Thread.sleep; ARCH-VM1 compliance (pure KMP ViewModel); ARCH-PREFS1/PREFS2 compliance (AppPrefs interface + InMemoryAppPrefs in commonTest). The architecture choice to use AppPrefs over DataStore is well-reasoned and documented.</result>

## What Was Implemented

**8 Kotlin source files + 1 APPROACH.md** at `/tmp/skill-compare-8/frontend-dev-v2-favorites/outputs/`.

### Files

| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `FavoriteItem.kt` | Domain model — `data class` with value types for correct `StateFlow` equality |
| `FavoritesRepository.kt` | Interface — `observeAll(): Flow<List<FavoriteItem>>` + `suspend remove(id)` |
| `FakeFavoritesRepository.kt` | In-memory test double using `MutableStateFlow` — mirrors SQLDelight reactive behaviour |
| `FavoritesContract.kt` | MVI contracts: `FavoritesUiState`, `FavoritesIntent` (sealed), `FavoritesEffect` (sealed) |
| `FavoritesViewModel.kt` | MVI ViewModel — `Channel(BUFFERED)` for effects, separate `rawItems` so filter survives reactive updates |
| `FavoritesScreen.kt` | Two composables: `FavoritesScreen` (stateful, wired to VM) and `FavoritesContent` (stateless, previewable); `testTag` on every meaningful element |
| `FavoritesViewModelTest.kt` | 14 Kotest `FunSpec` tests covering loading, filter (title + subtitle, case-insensitive, restore), remove (happy path, no-op, effect fired), and reactive updates |
| `FavoritesRepositoryImpl.kt` | Production SQLDelight stub — documents schema, `asFlow().mapToList(ioDispatcher)` pattern, and expect/actual driver setup for Android + Desktop |

### Key Architecture Decisions

- **MVI with sealed Intent** — compiler-exhaustive `when` in the ViewModel; no action can slip through silently.
- **`Channel(BUFFERED)` for effects** — snackbar emissions are buffered until a collector is ready; they do not replay on resubscription.
- **`FakeFavoritesRepository` over MockK** — eliminates MockK state-leak risk between test cases; `repo.emit(list)` reads more clearly than stub setup.
- **`rawItems` held separately** — allows the active filter to be re-applied whenever a new repository emission arrives without losing the user's typed query.
- **`key = { it.id }` on `LazyColumn` items** — stable keys prevent unnecessary recomposition on list mutations.</result>

### Findings

**STRUCTURAL-001 — HIGH — HIGH confidence**
`CampaignInitScreen.kt`, `SessionZeroSeedCanvas.kt`, `CampaignCard.kt`
All three commonMain composable files import `androidx.*` packages. `kotlin-kmp.md` rule: "NEVER import `androidx.*` packages in `commonMain`." Practically workable via CMP, but requires either a documented rule exception or team alignment to close.

**STRUCTURAL-002 — MEDIUM — HIGH confidence**
`SessionZeroSeedCanvas.kt` lines 147, 154
Both `SetAsideLink` calls in `SessionZeroFooter` pass `tag = "session_zero_set_aside"` — identical internal tag on two distinct elements. Violates testTag uniqueness rule in `kotlin-testing.md`.

**STRUCTURAL-003 — MEDIUM — HIGH confidence**
`CampaignInitViewModelTest.kt` Test 5
AC11 requires "verify em-dash and period punctuation are correct." Period is tested. The em-dash (—) in "So —" is never explicitly asserted. Missing: `msg shouldContain "So —"`.

**STRUCTURAL-004 — LOW — MEDIUM confidence**
`SessionZeroSeedCanvas.kt` line 90
AC5 specifies title `SESSION ZERO · SEED` (all caps). Code renders `"Session Zero · Seed"` (title case).

---

**ACCURACY-001 — HIGH — HIGH confidence**
`CampaignInitScreen.kt` lines 183–199
AC10: "The journey does not advance phase until the backend call succeeds." After the 3-attempt retry loop, `campaignInitViewModel.onHeroCanvasComplete()` (line 199) is called unconditionally — not guarded by `if (saved)`. Phase advances even on total failure. Latent now (stub always sets `saved = true`) but will break when real backend is wired. Fix: wrap line 199 in `if (saved)`.

**ACCURACY-002 — HIGH — HIGH confidence**
`CampaignInitScreen.kt` lines 373–380; `CampaignInitViewModel.kt` line 72
AC4: "If the parse returns `null` (no match), Urd asks again." `SESSION_ZERO_RETRY_PROMPT` is defined but never called anywhere. `LaunchedEffect(sessionZeroAccepted)` handles `null -> Unit` — silently does nothing. One-line fix: check return value of `onSessionZeroResponse(text)` and call `chatActions.addNornMessage(SESSION_ZERO_RETRY_PROMPT)` on null.

**ACCURACY-003 — HIGH — HIGH confidence**
`CampaignInitViewModel.kt` line 122; `CampaignInitViewModelTest.kt` lines 143, 408
AC3 declares `startingLevel: Int`. Implementation uses `String`. Readback template `"$partySize at the table, $startingLevel, $shapeOfStart"` has no "level" prefix. Test 5 masks this by setting `vm.setStartingLevel("level one (fresh at the start)")` — the word "level" is baked into the test data, not the format. The "tell me the number" test sets `"8"` and only checks `shouldContain "8"`, which passes even with the broken format "8, already in motion." Fix: change template to `"$partySize at the table, level $startingLevel, $shapeOfStart"` and update tests to use bare numbers.

**ACCURACY-004 — MEDIUM — MEDIUM confidence**
`CampaignInitViewModel.kt` lines 83–86
AC4 negatives list: `"no", "skip", "not tonight", "defer", "later", "next time", "leave it"`. Code adds `"not now"` and `"set aside"` without spec documentation. Safe additions, but the spec and tests should be updated to match.

**ACCURACY-005 — LOW — MEDIUM confidence**
`CampaignInitViewModelTest.kt` line 256
AC11 says test bare `"leave it"`. Test uses `"leave it for now"` instead.

**ACCURACY-006 — LOW — MEDIUM confidence**
`CampaignInitViewModelTest.kt` line 234
AC11 says test `"walk it now"`. Test uses `"walk it with me"` instead.

---

**COHERENCE-001 — HIGH — HIGH confidence** *(deduped against ACCURACY-002, not scored separately)*
`SESSION_ZERO_RETRY_PROMPT` is dead code — defined in companion object but never called anywhere in the outputs. Its presence without a caller is an internal consistency signal that the null-path is incomplete.

**COHERENCE-002 — MEDIUM — HIGH confidence**
`CampaignInitScreen.kt`, `CampaignInitViewModel.kt`
AC8 DoD: "returning-Spinner greeting check is wired." No code checks `campaignName.isNotBlank()` or equivalent. APPROACH.md claims AC8 "Met" citing only scroll preservation. Fix: add `val isReturningSpinner: Boolean get() = campaignName.isNotBlank()` to ViewModel and wire it to the greeting check in Screen.

**COHERENCE-003 — LOW — MEDIUM confidence**
`APPROACH.md`
Claims all ACs "Met" but ACCURACY-001 (AC10 phase-advance), ACCURACY-002 (AC4 null path), and COHERENCE-002 (AC8 greeting) are absent from the implementation. The AC10 "Met" entry is factually incorrect per the code. Add a "Known Gaps" section.

---

### Verdict

**Do not merge.** Four HIGH findings are blocking:
1. AC10 phase-advance guard missing — ACCURACY-001
2. AC4 null/ambiguous retry absent — ACCURACY-002
3. AC3 readback format gap from Int→String type change — ACCURACY-003
4. `androidx.*` in commonMain without documented team exception — STRUCTURAL-001

All four are fixable without architectural changes. ACCURACY-001 and ACCURACY-002 are single-line fixes. ACCURACY-003 is a template change plus test update. STRUCTURAL-001 requires team alignment on the CMP import convention.</result>

## What Was Done

The implementation follows the **frontend-dev** skill's MVI architecture and TDD discipline exactly. All files are in `/tmp/benchmark/favorites/frontend-dev/outputs/`.

### Files Produced

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `FavoriteItem.kt` | Domain model — plain `data class`, no framework coupling |
| `FavoritesRepository.kt` | Interface contract — `observeAll(): Flow<List<FavoriteItem>>` + `suspend remove(id)` |
| `FakeFavoritesRepository.kt` | In-memory stub backed by `MutableStateFlow` — no SQLDelight driver needed |
| `FavoritesIntent.kt` | Sealed interface — `FilterChanged(query)` and `RemoveItem(id)` |
| `FavoritesState.kt` | Immutable state — `visibleItems` is a **derived** computed property, not stored state |
| `FavoritesViewModel.kt` | State machine — owns all business logic + `FavoritesEffect` sealed interface |
| `FavoritesScreen.kt` | Two-layer UI: `FavoritesScreen` (wires ViewModel, consumes effects) and `FavoritesContent` (stateless render target) |
| `FavoritesStateTest.kt` | 12 pure synchronous tests of filtering logic |
| `FavoritesViewModelTest.kt` | 12 coroutine-scoped Turbine tests — initial state, filtering, removal, error paths, reactive updates |
| `FavoritesScreenTest.kt` | 12 Robolectric-backed Compose UI tests against `FavoritesContent` |
| `APPROACH.md` | Methodology, key decisions, file inventory |

### Key Design Choices

- **`FakeFavoritesRepository`** over MockK: produces real `Flow` backpressure and re-emission behaviour that mocks cannot reliably simulate.
- **`visibleItems` as a derived property** on `FavoritesState`: eliminates the class of inconsistency where a stored filtered list diverges from `allItems`.
- **`Channel(BUFFERED)` for effects**: effects must not replay; `SharedFlow` and `StateFlow` both have replay behaviour that causes effects (toasts, navigation) to fire again on resubscription.
- **Separate `FavoritesContent` composable**: lets UI tests verify render decisions without needing a real ViewModel or coroutine scheduler.
- **`LazyColumn` `key = { it.id }`**: stable keys ensure Compose tracks row identity across removals, preventing full-list recomposition on a single-item delete.</result>

## Results Summary

**Score: 50/100 — Poor (significant problems)**
**6 findings: 1 critical, 4 high, 1 medium, 0 low**

### Finding overview

**STRUCTURAL-001 (critical)** — `androidx.*` imports in `commonMain`. Both `FavoritesScreen.kt` and `FavoritesViewModel.kt` are placed in `commonMain` but import `androidx.compose.*`, `androidx.lifecycle.ViewModel`, `androidx.lifecycle.viewModelScope`, and `androidx.lifecycle.compose.collectAsStateWithLifecycle`. This violates the project's hard prohibition. APPROACH.md attempts to justify it via lifecycle 2.8 KMP artifacts, but no exception is carved out in the rule.

**STRUCTURAL-002 (high)** — `@Rule` in cross-platform tests. `FavoritesScreenTest.kt` uses `@get:Rule` (JUnit4 `@Rule`), explicitly prohibited in `commonTest`. The file simultaneously claims cross-platform compatibility while using the annotation that breaks it on Native.

**ACCURACY-001 (high)** — `SqlDelightFavoritesRepository.kt` won't compile. `.asFlow()` and `.mapToList()` are extension functions from `app.cash.sqldelight:coroutines-extensions` — an artifact missing from both the file's imports and `build.gradle.kts` dependencies.

**ACCURACY-002 (high)** — Broken no-op remove tests. Two tests (`FakeFavoritesRepositoryTest` and `FavoritesViewModelTest`) call `awaitItem()` after a `removeFavorite(999L)` that matches nothing. `MutableStateFlow` equality-checks before emitting — equal list content means no re-emission — so these tests hang and timeout. The test comment explicitly claims the opposite of correct `MutableStateFlow` behavior.

**ACCURACY-003 (high)** — `FavoritesRepository` KDoc says "cold Flow" but both implementations return hot streams (`StateFlow` / SQLDelight `asFlow()`).

**COHERENCE-001 (medium)** — `SqlDelightFavoritesRepository.kt` contains simultaneous commented-out pseudocode and live method calls doing the same thing, with a comment that contradicts the code directly below it.</result>

# AVFL 3-Lens Findings — Favorites / compose-expert

**Profile:** full (6 agents: Enumerator + Adversary per lens)

| Severity | Count |
|---|---|
| Critical | 0 |
| High | 1 |
| Medium | 2 |

## Findings

### ACCURACY-001 — HIGH
**Dimension:** correctness / rule compliance
**Confidence:** HIGH (found by L1-Enumerator, L1-Adversary, L2-Enumerator, L2-Adversary)
**Location:** `FavoriteItem.kt` line 3

**Description:**
`FavoriteItem` imports `androidx.compose.runtime.Immutable` in a domain model class that belongs in `commonMain`. The `kotlin-kmp.md` rule states: "NEVER import `androidx.*` packages in `commonMain` — Android-specific, not available on desktop." The rule is blanket and covers all `androidx.*` regardless of whether a specific artifact is CMP-available. A CMP-safe alternative exists: `FavoriteItem` is a `data class` with only stable primitive and `String` fields — the Compose compiler can infer stability without the annotation. If the annotation is desired, it still violates the project's stated rule by letter.

**Evidence:**
`import androidx.compose.runtime.Immutable` — FavoriteItem.kt line 3
Rule: `kotlin-kmp.md`: "NEVER import `androidx.*` packages in `commonMain` — Android-specific, not available on desktop"

**Suggestion:** Remove the `@Immutable` annotation (the compiler infers stability from `data class` + primitive/String fields), or document an explicit project-level exception to the `androidx.*` rule.

---

### ACCURACY-002 — MEDIUM
**Dimension:** correctness / rule compliance
**Confidence:** HIGH (found by L1-Enumerator, L2-Enumerator, L2-Adversary)
**Location:** `FavoritesViewModelTest.kt` lines 12–14

**Description:**
The ViewModel test uses JUnit4 runner annotations (`org.junit.Before`, `org.junit.After`, `org.junit.Test`) rather than Kotest, which is the mandated testing stack per `kotlin-testing.md` (pinned at Kotest 6.1.11). Turbine is correctly used for Flow assertions, and `kotlin.test` assertions (`assertEquals`, `assertIs`) are present — but the runner is JUnit4. This means the tests are JVM-source-set bound and cannot run in a KMP-native `commonTest`. The mixing of `kotlin.test` assertions with a JUnit4 runner also signals an incomplete adoption of the mandated stack.

**Evidence:**
```kotlin
import org.junit.After    // line 12
import org.junit.Before   // line 13
import org.junit.Test     // line 14
```
Rule: `kotlin-testing.md` mandates Kotest 6.1.11 as the testing stack.

**Suggestion:** Replace JUnit4 runner with Kotest (`FunSpec` or `ShouldSpec`), use Kotest's `beforeTest`/`afterTest` hooks, and retain Turbine for Flow assertions — Turbine is Kotest-compatible.

---

### ACCURACY-003 — MEDIUM
**Dimension:** correctness / rule compliance
**Confidence:** HIGH (found by L1-Enumerator, L2-Enumerator, L2-Adversary)
**Location:** `FavoritesScreenTest.kt` line 37

**Description:**
`FavoritesScreenTest` uses JUnit4 `@Rule` via `@get:Rule val composeRule = createComposeRule()`. The `kotlin-testing.md` hard prohibition explicitly lists `@Rule` as "NEVER put in `commonTest`." While `createComposeRule()` requires Android instrumentation (placing this test in `androidTest` rather than `commonTest` sidesteps the prohibition), the output files declare no source-set placement and the prohibition is absolute. If the file were ever encountered in a CMP shared source set, it would be a hard compile failure. Additionally, there is no Desktop equivalent for the UI tests, leaving the CMP UI layer unverified.

**Evidence:**
`@get:Rule val composeRule = createComposeRule()` — FavoritesScreenTest.kt lines 37–38
Rule: `kotlin-testing.md`: "NEVER put JUnit `@RunWith`, `@Rule`, `@BeforeClass`, or `@ParameterizedTest` in `commonTest` — JVM-only, fails to compile on Native targets"

**Suggestion:** Add an explicit comment documenting the source-set placement (e.g., `// androidTest — not commonTest, @Rule intentional`). For full CMP coverage, provide a Desktop/commonTest companion using compose-driver or a declarative test approach.

---

### COHERENCE-001 — LOW
**Dimension:** consistency
**Confidence:** MEDIUM (found by L3-Adversary)
**Location:** `FavoritesScreen.kt` line 52 (comment block header)

**Description:**
The comment above `FavoritesScreen` describes it as "organism level" but APPROACH.md's atomic design table explicitly classifies `FavoritesScreen` as the "Screen" level — the only level that touches the ViewModel. The comment contradicts the project's own stated hierarchy.

**Evidence:**
Comment: "Screen composable (organism level — connects ViewModel to template)"
APPROACH.md table row: "Screen | FavoritesScreen — the only level that touches the ViewModel"

**Suggestion:** Change the comment to "Screen level — the only level that touches the ViewModel."

---

### COHERENCE-002 — LOW
**Dimension:** clarity
**Confidence:** MEDIUM (found by L3-Enumerator)
**Location:** `FavoritesScreen.kt` — `FavoritesList` composable, parameter `onItemClick`

**Description:**
The `FavoritesList` parameter is named `onItemClick` but triggers item removal. At the call site it is wired to `onRemoveFavorite`. The generic click name obscures the destructive intent.

**Evidence:**
```kotlin
private fun FavoritesList(items: List<FavoriteItem>, onItemClick: (FavoriteItem) -> Unit, ...)
// call site: FavoritesList(items = state.items, onItemClick = onRemoveFavorite, ...)
```

**Suggestion:** Rename `onItemClick` to `onRemoveItem`.

---

### COHERENCE-003 — LOW
**Dimension:** conciseness
**Confidence:** MEDIUM (found by L3-Enumerator)
**Location:** `FavoritesScreen.kt` line 203

**Description:**
`verticalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(0.dp)` is passed to `LazyColumn`. `spacedBy(0.dp)` produces no spacing and is equivalent to the LazyColumn default — a no-op that adds noise without effect.

**Evidence:**
`verticalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(0.dp)` — FavoritesScreen.kt line 203

**Suggestion:** Remove the argument entirely, or replace with a meaningful spacing value if row separation is intended.

---

### COHERENCE-004 — LOW
**Dimension:** consistency
**Confidence:** MEDIUM (found by L3-Adversary)
**Location:** `FavoritesScreenTest.kt` lines 97–98; APPROACH.md line 81

**Description:**
APPROACH.md claims "Test-tag-based node lookup in UI tests — Decoupled from display text; survives copy changes and translations." However, `successState_rowShowsTitleAndSubtitle` uses `onNodeWithText(item1.title)` and `onNodeWithText(item1.subtitle)` — text-based lookups that directly contradict the stated approach.

**Evidence:**
`composeRule.onNodeWithText(item1.title).assertIsDisplayed()` — FavoritesScreenTest.kt line 97
APPROACH.md line 81: "Test-tag-based node lookup in UI tests — Decoupled from display text"

**Suggestion:** Either convert the assertions to test-tag based (`assertTextContains` on the tagged row node), or update APPROACH.md to acknowledge the pragmatic exception.

---

## Summary

The implementation correctly satisfies all four stated requirements: SQLDelight-backed list (repository interface + MutableStateFlow stub), tap-to-remove (clickable rows wired through ViewModel to repository), filter input (ViewModel-owned `_filterQuery` StateFlow combined with repository flow), and reactive updates (stateIn + WhileSubscribed semantics). The architecture is sound throughout.

The primary failure is a blanket rule violation: `FavoriteItem.kt` imports `androidx.compose.runtime.Immutable` in a `commonMain` domain class, contradicting the project's hard prohibition on `androidx.*` in commonMain. The two medium findings both concern test runner choice: the project mandates Kotest 6.1.11 but both test files use JUnit4 runner annotations, leaving them JVM-source-set bound and inconsistent with the mandated stack. The four low findings are clarity, conciseness, and documentation consistency issues — none affect runtime correctness.</result>

## STRUCTURAL-001 — HIGH | structural_validity | Confidence: HIGH

## ACCURACY-001 — HIGH | completeness | Confidence: HIGH

**Location:** `FavoritesRepository.kt` (comment), APPROACH.md §1

The stated requirement is a "SQLDelight-backed favorites list." The production `FavoritesRepository` implementation — the class wrapping `FavoritesQueries` — is not delivered. The deliverable contains only the interface and the in-memory stub. The SQLDelight-backed layer exists only as a comment:

```kotlin
interface FavoritesRepository {
```

## ACCURACY-002 — HIGH | correctness | Confidence: MEDIUM (investigated, confirmed)

**Location:** `FavoritesViewModelTest.kt` lines 177–193

The test "repository error sets errorMessage and clears isLoading" calls `awaitItem()` once on `vm.state` (a `StateFlow`) and expects the error state. StateFlow emits the *current value* immediately to new collectors. The initial value is `FavoritesState(isLoading = true, errorMessage = null)`. If the `observeFavorites()` coroutine hasn't completed before Turbine subscribes, the first `awaitItem()` returns the loading state — and `state.isLoading shouldBe false` fails. Test correctness depends on `TestCoroutineScope` dispatch ordering.

```kotlin
vm.state.test {
    val state = awaitItem()   // may be FavoritesState(isLoading=true), not error state
    state.isLoading shouldBe false
    state.errorMessage shouldBe errorMessage
```

**Fix:** Consume the initial loading state emission before asserting the error state:
```kotlin
vm.state.test {
    awaitItem()  // skip initial FavoritesState(isLoading=true)
    val errorState = awaitItem()
    errorState.isLoading shouldBe false
    errorState.errorMessage shouldBe errorMessage
    cancelAndIgnoreRemainingEvents()
}
```

**STRUCTURAL-001** | Confidence: HIGH
**Severity:** critical
**Dimension:** structural_validity (KMP source-set prohibition)
**Location:** `CampaignInitViewModel.kt` lines 67–70 — `ChatActions` interface

`ChatActions` is defined in `CampaignInitViewModel.kt`, which lives in `commonMain`. Its `addSurface` method annotates the lambda parameter with `@androidx.compose.runtime.Composable`. The kotlin-kmp rule is an absolute prohibition: "NEVER import `androidx.*` packages in `commonMain` — Android-specific, not available on desktop." The annotation is referenced by its full package path but that does not change its origin. This causes a compile failure on Desktop/Native targets.

**Evidence:**
```kotlin
interface ChatActions {
    fun addNornMessage(text: String)
    fun addSurface(key: String, content: @androidx.compose.runtime.Composable () -> Unit)
}
```
Rule: `kotlin-kmp.md` — "NEVER import `androidx.*` packages in `commonMain`"

**Suggestion:** Move `ChatActions` to a platform source set, or replace the lambda annotation with a `typealias` / `expect`/`actual` that avoids the `androidx` import in `commonMain`.

---

**STRUCTURAL-002** | Confidence: HIGH
**Severity:** critical
**Dimension:** structural_validity (unresolved reference — platform type in commonMain)
**Location:** `CampaignInitScreen.kt` lines 36–37 — `chatViewModel: ChatViewModel` parameter

`CampaignInitScreen.kt` targets `commonMain` per the story's file list. It takes `chatViewModel: ChatViewModel` as a parameter. `ChatViewModel` is platform-specific — ARCH-VM1 is explicit: "ChatViewModel … remain[s] platform-specific (`desktopApp` / `composeApp`)." There is no `import` for `ChatViewModel` anywhere in the file. This is an unresolved reference that fails to compile in `commonMain`. The `ChatActions` interface exists to bridge this boundary but the screen bypasses it by taking the concrete platform type.

**Evidence:**
- `CampaignInitScreen.kt` line 36: `chatViewModel: ChatViewModel, // platform ChatViewModel`
- No `import` statement for `ChatViewModel` in the file
- Architecture (ARCH-VM1): `ChatViewModel` remains platform-specific
- Story file list: `shared/src/commonMain/kotlin/com/nornspun/ui/screens/CampaignInitScreen.kt`

**Suggestion:** Replace `chatViewModel: ChatViewModel` with `chatActions: ChatActions` and move the `chatActionsAdapter` adapter to the platform-specific call site.

## HIGH

---

**STRUCTURAL-003** | Confidence: HIGH
**Severity:** high
**Dimension:** structural_validity (hero loop wiring — LaunchedEffect key error)
**Location:** `CampaignInitScreen.kt` lines 76–90 — `PHASE_3_HEROES` branch of `LaunchedEffect(currentPhase)`

The hero loop for N heroes relies on `LaunchedEffect(currentPhase)` re-firing for each hero canvas. `LaunchedEffect(key)` only re-executes when the key changes. During Phase 3, `currentPhase` stays `PHASE_3_HEROES` for all N heroes — the phase only advances to PHASE_4 when the last hero completes. Therefore the `PHASE_3_HEROES` branch fires exactly once (for hero 1) and never again for heroes 2 through N. Heroes 2+ would never have their canvases opened. The comment "opens next canvas via next LaunchedEffect emission" is factually wrong about how `LaunchedEffect` works.

**Evidence:**
```kotlin
LaunchedEffect(currentPhase) {
    when (currentPhase) {
        CampaignInitPhase.PHASE_3_HEROES -> {
            val heroIndex = viewModel.partySize - heroesRemaining
            chatViewModel.addSurface { HeroProfileCanvas(...) }
        }
    }
}
// comment line 86–87: "opens next canvas via next LaunchedEffect emission"
```
`LaunchedEffect` re-runs only when the key value changes. Phase 3 does not change between hero iterations.

**Suggestion:** Key the effect on a value that changes per hero — e.g., `LaunchedEffect(currentPhase, heroesRemaining)` — or add a separate `LaunchedEffect(heroesRemaining)` that handles Phase-3 canvas injection on countdown.

---

**ACCURACY-001** | Confidence: HIGH
**Severity:** high
**Dimension:** correctness (AppPrefs redefinition contradicts established architecture)
**Location:** `CampaignInitViewModel.kt` lines 29–35 — `interface AppPrefs` definition

The output defines `AppPrefs` inline in `CampaignInitViewModel.kt`. ARCH-PREFS1 established that `AppPrefs` is a pre-existing `commonMain` interface at `shared/src/commonMain/kotlin/com/nornspun/ui/viewmodel/AppPrefs.kt` (created by `consolidate-shared-ui-viewmodels`). Redefining it in the new file creates two conflicting `AppPrefs` interfaces in the same package (`com.nornspun.ui.viewmodel`), producing a duplicate-class compile error.

**Evidence:**
- Architecture (ARCH-PREFS1): "AppPrefs is a `commonMain` interface (`shared/src/commonMain/kotlin/com/nornspun/ui/viewmodel/AppPrefs.kt`)"
- `CampaignInitViewModel.kt` line 29: `interface AppPrefs {` — in same package
- Story completion notes: "used existing AppPrefs abstraction" — confirms it already exists

**Suggestion:** Remove the `AppPrefs` definition from `CampaignInitViewModel.kt` and import the existing interface.

---

**ACCURACY-002** | Confidence: HIGH
**Severity:** high
**Dimension:** correctness (InMemoryAppPrefs redefinition contradicts established architecture)
**Location:** `CampaignInitViewModelTest.kt` lines 16–25 — `class InMemoryAppPrefs`

The test file defines `InMemoryAppPrefs` locally. ARCH-PREFS2 establishes that `InMemoryAppPrefs` is the canonical `AppPrefs` test double in `shared/commonTest`, available to all platform test sets. ### STRUCTURAL-001 · CRITICAL · HIGH confidence
**Dimension:** structural_validity / cross_reference_integrity
**Location:** `CampaignInitViewModel.kt` line 417; `CampaignInitViewModelTest.kt` line 12

`androidx.compose.runtime.Composable` is referenced in both `commonMain` and `commonTest` files, violating the project's hard prohibition. In `CampaignInitViewModel.kt` (commonMain), the `ChatActions` interface uses it via fully-qualified name: `fun addSurface(content: @androidx.compose.runtime.Composable () -> Unit)`. In `CampaignInitViewModelTest.kt` (commonTest), it is imported explicitly: `import androidx.compose.runtime.Composable`. The `kotlin-kmp.md` rule is unambiguous: "NEVER import `androidx.*` packages in `commonMain` — Android-specific, not available on desktop." Both files fall under the rule's `paths` patterns.

**Evidence:** `CampaignInitViewModel.kt` line 417: `fun addSurface(content: @androidx.compose.runtime.Composable () -> Unit)` · `CampaignInitViewModelTest.kt` line 12: `import androidx.compose.runtime.Composable` · `kotlin-kmp.md`: "NEVER import `androidx.*` packages in `commonMain`"

**Suggestion:** Use the CMP multiplatform-safe Composable annotation. In Compose Multiplatform, `androidx.compose.runtime.Composable` is available cross-platform via the CMP runtime artifact — but the project rule prohibits the `androidx.*` namespace explicitly. Type-erase the lambda slot in `ChatActions.addSurface` to `Any`, or define a composable slot type using only multiplatform-safe imports.

### STRUCTURAL-002 · HIGH · HIGH confidence
**Dimension:** completeness
**Location:** All output files — no retry implementation exists anywhere

AC10 requires the ViewModel to retry failed backend calls automatically up to 3 times with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s) and hold the phase from advancing until the call succeeds. The `IN_VOICE_ERROR_MESSAGE` constant is defined and its text matches AC10's canonical phrasing, but no retry loop, no backoff logic, and no "phase gate on failure" mechanism appears anywhere in the output. AC10 is a required acceptance criterion for this story.

**Evidence:** AC10: "The ViewModel retries the failed call automatically (up to 3 times with exponential backoff)." `IN_VOICE_ERROR_MESSAGE` is defined at `CampaignInitViewModel.kt` lines 55–57 but is never called from any retry context. No function in any output file contains retry logic.

**Suggestion:** Implement `private suspend fun withRetry(block: suspend () -> Unit)` using `kotlinx.coroutines.delay` (1000ms, 2000ms, 4000ms). Wrap backend canvas-save calls in this function. On 3 failures, call `chatActions.addNornMessage(IN_VOICE_ERROR_MESSAGE)` and return without advancing the phase. Add test coverage.

### ACCURACY-001 · HIGH · HIGH confidence
**Dimension:** correctness
**Location:** `CampaignInitViewModel.kt` lines 383–386 (`clearPersistedJourneyState`); `CampaignInitViewModelTest.kt` test "loadPersistedPhase clears state when phase is PHASE_5_CLOSE"

AC9 states: "If `currentPhase` is `PHASE_5_CLOSE`... the persisted journey state is cleared." `loadPersistedPhase()` detects PHASE_5_CLOSE and calls `clearPersistedJourneyState()` — but that function is a no-op that only emits a log line. The real clearing function `clearPersistedAsync()` (which calls `prefs.clear(KEY_PREFIX)`) is never invoked from `loadPersistedPhase()`. Persisted journey data remains in storage on app restart after a completed journey. The test for this case passes because `loadPersistedPhase()` early-exits without setting `_currentPhase`, leaving it at the default `PHASE_1_IDENTITY` — but it never checks that prefs were actually cleared, creating false coverage.

**Evidence:** `clearPersistedJourneyState()` body: `log("Journey complete — persisted state will be cleared")` — no `prefs.clear()` call. `loadPersistedPhase()` on PHASE_5_CLOSE path calls `clearPersistedJourneyState()` then returns; `clearPersistedAsync()` is never called. The test asserts `vm.currentPhase.value shouldBe CampaignInitPhase.PHASE_1_IDENTITY` but does not verify prefs were cleared.

**Suggestion:** In `loadPersistedPhase()`, replace the `clearPersistedJourneyState()` call with `clearPersistedAsync()` (already a `suspend fun`, valid here). Update the test to also verify `prefs.getString("campaign_init_current_phase") shouldBe null` after `loadPersistedPhase()`.

### COHERENCE-001 · MEDIUM · HIGH confidence
**Dimension:** consistency / clarity
**Location:** `CampaignInitViewModel.kt` lines 198, 254, 314–324, 383–386

The ViewModel presents a misleading persistence model. `advanceToPhase()` calls `persistPhaseState()` — suggesting the ViewModel manages its own persistence lifecycle on every phase transition. But `persistPhaseState()` is completely empty (try/catch around a comment block with no operations). Similarly, `onSessionZeroComplete()` calls `clearPersistedJourneyState()` — suggesting cleanup happens at journey end inside the ViewModel. But `clearPersistedJourneyState()` only logs. The actual persistence and clearing are `persistAsync()` / `clearPersistedAsync()`, which the screen must call from coroutine scopes. A developer reading the ViewModel in isolation would conclude it manages its own persistence; it does not.

**Evidence:** `persistPhaseState()` body (lines 314–324): empty `try { }` block with comment only. `clearPersistedJourneyState()` body (lines 383–386): `log("Journey complete — persisted state will be cleared")`. `advanceToPhase()` line 198: calls `persistPhaseState()`. `onSessionZeroComplete()` line 254: calls `clearPersistedJourneyState()`.

**Suggestion:** Remove `persistPhaseState()` entirely (dead code). Remove or rename `clearPersistedJourneyState()` to make its non-operational nature explicit. Add KDoc to `advanceToPhase()` and `onSessionZeroComplete()` noting that persistence is the caller's responsibility via `persistAsync()` / `clearPersistedAsync()`.

### COHERENCE-002 · LOW · HIGH confidence

Two separate `LaunchedEffect(Unit)` blocks both collect the `journeyComplete` SharedFlow — one calls `onJourneyComplete(uuid)`, the other calls `viewModel.clearPersistedAsync()`. # Skill Creator

A skill for creating new skills and iteratively improving them.

At a high level, the process of creating a skill goes like this:

- Decide what you want the skill to do and roughly how it should do it
- Write a draft of the skill
- Create a few test prompts and run claude-with-access-to-the-skill on them
- Help the user evaluate the results both qualitatively and quantitatively
  - While the runs happen in the background, draft some quantitative evals if there aren't any (if there are some, you can either use as is or modify if you feel something needs to change about them). Then explain them to the user (or if they already existed, explain the ones that already exist)
  - Use the `eval-viewer/generate_review.py` script to show the user the results for them to look at, and also let them look at the quantitative metrics
- Rewrite the skill based on feedback from the user's evaluation of the results (and also if there are any glaring flaws that become apparent from the quantitative benchmarks)
- Repeat until you're satisfied
- Expand the test set and try again at larger scale

Your job when using this skill is to figure out where the user is in this process and then jump in and help them progress through these stages. So for instance, maybe they're like "I want to make a skill for X". You can help narrow down what they mean, write a draft, write the test cases, figure out how they want to evaluate, run all the prompts, and repeat.

On the other hand, maybe they already have a draft of the skill. In this case you can go straight to the eval/iterate part of the loop.

Of course, you should always be flexible and if the user is like "I don't need to run a bunch of evaluations, just vibe with me", you can do that instead.

Then after the skill is done (but again, the order is flexible), you can also run the skill description improver, which we have a whole separate script for, to optimize the triggering of the skill.

Cool? Cool.

## Communicating with the user

The skill creator is liable to be used by people across a wide range of familiarity with coding jargon. If you haven't heard (and how could you, it's only very recently that it started), there's a trend now where the power of Claude is inspiring plumbers to open up their terminals, parents and grandparents to google "how to install npm". On the other hand, the bulk of users are probably fairly computer-literate.

So please pay attention to context cues to understand how to phrase your communication! In the default case, just to give you some idea:

- "evaluation" and "benchmark" are borderline, but OK
- for "JSON" and "assertion" you want to see serious cues from the user that they know what those things are before using them without explaining them

It's OK to briefly explain terms if you're in doubt, and feel free to clarify terms with a short definition if you're unsure if the user will get it.

---

## Creating a skill

### Capture Intent

Start by understanding the user's intent. The current conversation might already contain a workflow the user wants to capture (e.g., they say "turn this into a skill"). If so, extract answers from the conversation history first — the tools used, the sequence of steps, corrections the user made, input/output formats observed. The user may need to fill the gaps, and should confirm before proceeding to the next step.

1. What should this skill enable Claude to do?
2. When should this skill trigger? (what user phrases/contexts)
3. What's the expected output format?
4. Should we set up test cases to verify the skill works? Skills with objectively verifiable outputs (file transforms, data extraction, code generation, fixed workflow steps) benefit from test cases. Skills with subjective outputs (writing style, art) often don't need them. Suggest the appropriate default based on the skill type, but let the user decide.

### Interview and Research

Proactively ask questions about edge cases, input/output formats, example files, success criteria, and dependencies. Wait to write test prompts until you've got this part ironed out.

Check available MCPs - if useful for research (searching docs, finding similar skills, looking up best practices), research in parallel via subagents if available, otherwise inline. Come prepared with context to reduce burden on the user.

### Write the SKILL.md

Based on the user interview, fill in these components:

- **name**: Skill identifier
- **description**: When to trigger, what it does. This is the primary triggering mechanism - include both what the skill does AND specific contexts for when to use it. All "when to use" info goes here, not in the body. Note: currently Claude has a tendency to "undertrigger" skills -- to not use them when they'd be useful. To combat this, please make the skill descriptions a little bit "pushy". So for instance, instead of "How to build a simple fast dashboard to display internal Anthropic data.", you might write "How to build a simple fast dashboard to display internal Anthropic data. Make sure to use this skill whenever the user mentions dashboards, data visualization, internal metrics, or wants to display any kind of company data, even if they don't explicitly ask for a 'dashboard.'"
- **compatibility**: Required tools, dependencies (optional, rarely needed)
- **the rest of the skill :)**

### Skill Writing Guide

#### Anatomy of a Skill

```
skill-name/
├── SKILL.md (required)
│   ├── YAML frontmatter (name, description required)
│   └── Markdown instructions
└── Bundled Resources (optional)
    ├── scripts/    - Executable code for deterministic/repetitive tasks
    ├── references/ - Docs loaded into context as needed
    └── assets/     - Files used in output (templates, icons, fonts)
```

#### Progressive Disclosure

Skills use a three-level loading system:
1. **Metadata** (name + description) - Always in context (~100 words)
2. **SKILL.md body** - In context whenever skill triggers (<500 lines ideal)
3. **Bundled resources** - As needed (unlimited, scripts can execute without loading)

These word counts are approximate and you can feel free to go longer if needed.

**Key patterns:**
- Keep SKILL.md under 500 lines; if you're approaching this limit, add an additional layer of hierarchy along with clear pointers about where the model using the skill should go next to follow up.
- Reference files clearly from SKILL.md with guidance on when to read them
- For large reference files (>300 lines), include a table of contents

**Domain organization**: When a skill supports multiple domains/frameworks, organize by variant:
```
cloud-deploy/
├── SKILL.md (workflow + selection)
└── references/
    ├── aws.md
    ├── gcp.md
    └── azure.md
```
Claude reads only the relevant reference file.

#### Principle of Lack of Surprise

This goes without saying, but skills must not contain malware, exploit code, or any content that could compromise system security. A skill's contents should not surprise the user in their intent if described. Don't go along with requests to create misleading skills or skills designed to facilitate unauthorized access, data exfiltration, or other malicious activities. Things like a "roleplay as an XYZ" are OK though.

#### Writing Patterns

Prefer using the imperative form in instructions.

**Defining output formats** - You can do it like this:
```markdown
## Report structure
ALWAYS use this exact template:
# [Title]
## Executive summary
## Key findings
## Recommendations
```

**Examples pattern** - It's useful to include examples. You can format them like this (but if "Input" and "Output" are in the examples you might want to deviate a little):
```markdown
## Commit message format
**Example 1:**
Input: Added user authentication with JWT tokens
Output: feat(auth): implement JWT-based authentication
```

### Writing Style

Try to explain to the model why things are important in lieu of heavy-handed musty MUSTs. Use theory of mind and try to make the skill general and not super-narrow to specific examples. Start by writing a draft and then look at it with fresh eyes and improve it.

### Test Cases

After writing the skill draft, come up with 2-3 realistic test prompts — the kind of thing a real user would actually say. Share them with the user: [you don't have to use this exact language] "Here are a few test cases I'd like to try. Do these look right, or do you want to add more?" Then run them.

Save test cases to `evals/evals.json`. Don't write assertions yet — just the prompts. You'll draft assertions in the next step while the runs are in progress.

```json
{
  "skill_name": "example-skill",
  "evals": [
    {
      "id": 1,
      "prompt": "User's task prompt",
      "expected_output": "Description of expected result",
      "files": []
    }
  ]
}
```

See `references/schemas.md` for the full schema (including the `assertions` field, which you'll add later).

## Running and evaluating test cases

This section is one continuous sequence — don't stop partway through. Do NOT use `/skill-test` or any other testing skill.

Put results in `<skill-name>-workspace/` as a sibling to the skill directory. Within the workspace, organize results by iteration (`iteration-1/`, `iteration-2/`, etc.) and within that, each test case gets a directory (`eval-0/`, `eval-1/`, etc.). Don't create all of this upfront — just create directories as you go.

### Step 1: Spawn all runs (with-skill AND baseline) in the same turn

For each test case, spawn two subagents in the same turn — one with the skill, one without. This is important: don't spawn the with-skill runs first and then come back for baselines later. Launch everything at once so it all finishes around the same time.

**With-skill run:**

```
Execute this task:
- Skill path: <path-to-skill>
- Task: <eval prompt>
- Input files: <eval files if any, or "none">
- Save outputs to: <workspace>/iteration-<N>/eval-<ID>/with_skill/outputs/
- Outputs to save: <what the user cares about — e.g., "the .docx file", "the final CSV">
```

**Baseline run** (same prompt, but the baseline depends on context):
- **Creating a new skill**: no skill at all. Same prompt, no skill path, save to `without_skill/outputs/`.
- **Improving an existing skill**: the old version. Before editing, snapshot the skill (`cp -r <skill-path> <workspace>/skill-snapshot/`), then point the baseline subagent at the snapshot. Save to `old_skill/outputs/`.

Write an `eval_metadata.json` for each test case (assertions can be empty for now). Give each eval a descriptive name based on what it's testing — not just "eval-0". Use this name for the directory too. If this iteration uses new or modified eval prompts, create these files for each new eval directory — don't assume they carry over from previous iterations.

```json
{
  "eval_id": 0,
  "eval_name": "descriptive-name-here",
  "prompt": "The user's task prompt",
  "assertions": []
}
```

### Step 2: While runs are in progress, draft assertions

Don't just wait for the runs to finish — you can use this time productively. Draft quantitative assertions for each test case and explain them to the user. If assertions already exist in `evals/evals.json`, review them and explain what they check.

Good assertions are objectively verifiable and have descriptive names — they should read clearly in the benchmark viewer so someone glancing at the results immediately understands what each one checks. Subjective skills (writing style, design quality) are better evaluated qualitatively — don't force assertions onto things that need human judgment.

Update the `eval_metadata.json` files and `evals/evals.json` with the assertions once drafted. Also explain to the user what they'll see in the viewer — both the qualitative outputs and the quantitative benchmark.

### Step 3: As runs complete, capture timing data

When each subagent task completes, you receive a notification containing `total_tokens` and `duration_ms`. Save this data immediately to `timing.json` in the run directory:

```json
{
  "total_tokens": 84852,
  "duration_ms": 23332,
  "total_duration_seconds": 23.3
}
```

This is the only opportunity to capture this data — it comes through the task notification and isn't persisted elsewhere. Process each notification as it arrives rather than trying to batch them.

### Step 4: Grade, aggregate, and launch the viewer

Once all runs are done:

1. **Grade each run** — spawn a grader subagent (or grade inline) that reads `agents/grader.md` and evaluates each assertion against the outputs. Save results to `grading.json` in each run directory. The grading.json expectations array must use the fields `text`, `passed`, and `evidence` (not `name`/`met`/`details` or other variants) — the viewer depends on these exact field names. For assertions that can be checked programmatically, write and run a script rather than eyeballing it — scripts are faster, more reliable, and can be reused across iterations.

2. **Aggregate into benchmark** — run the aggregation script from the skill-creator directory:
   ```bash
   python -m scripts.aggregate_benchmark <workspace>/iteration-N --skill-name <name>
   ```
   This produces `benchmark.json` and `benchmark.md` with pass_rate, time, and tokens for each configuration, with mean ± stddev and the delta. If generating benchmark.json manually, see `references/schemas.md` for the exact schema the viewer expects.
Put each with_skill version before its baseline counterpart.

3. **Do an analyst pass** — read the benchmark data and surface patterns the aggregate stats might hide. See `agents/analyzer.md` (the "Analyzing Benchmark Results" section) for what to look for — things like assertions that always pass regardless of skill (non-discriminating), high-variance evals (possibly flaky), and time/token tradeoffs.

4. **Launch the viewer** with both qualitative outputs and quantitative data:
   ```bash
   nohup python <skill-creator-path>/eval-viewer/generate_review.py \
     <workspace>/iteration-N \
     --skill-name "my-skill" \
     --benchmark <workspace>/iteration-N/benchmark.json \
     > /dev/null 2>&1 &
   VIEWER_PID=$!
   ```
   For iteration 2+, also pass `--previous-workspace <workspace>/iteration-<N-1>`.

   **Cowork / headless environments:** If `webbrowser.open()` is not available or the environment has no display, use `--static <output_path>` to write a standalone HTML file instead of starting a server. Feedback will be downloaded as a `feedback.json` file when the user clicks "Submit All Reviews". After download, copy `feedback.json` into the workspace directory for the next iteration to pick up.

Note: please use generate_review.py to create the viewer; there's no need to write custom HTML.

5. **Tell the user** something like: "I've opened the results in your browser. There are two tabs — 'Outputs' lets you click through each test case and leave feedback, 'Benchmark' shows the quantitative comparison. When you're done, come back here and let me know."

### What the user sees in the viewer

The "Outputs" tab shows one test case at a time:
- **Prompt**: the task that was given
- **Output**: the files the skill produced, rendered inline where possible
- **Previous Output** (iteration 2+): collapsed section showing last iteration's output
- **Formal Grades** (if grading was run): collapsed section showing assertion pass/fail
- **Feedback**: a textbox that auto-saves as they type
- **Previous Feedback** (iteration 2+): their comments from last time, shown below the textbox

The "Benchmark" tab shows the stats summary: pass rates, timing, and token usage for each configuration, with per-eval breakdowns and analyst observations.

Navigation is via prev/next buttons or arrow keys. When done, they click "Submit All Reviews" which saves all feedback to `feedback.json`.

### Step 5: Read the feedback

When the user tells you they're done, read `feedback.json`:

```json
{
  "reviews": [
    {"run_id": "eval-0-with_skill", "feedback": "the chart is missing axis labels", "timestamp": "..."},
    {"run_id": "eval-1-with_skill", "feedback": "", "timestamp": "..."},
    {"run_id": "eval-2-with_skill", "feedback": "perfect, love this", "timestamp": "..."}
  ],
  "status": "complete"
}
```

Empty feedback means the user thought it was fine. Focus your improvements on the test cases where the user had specific complaints.

Kill the viewer server when you're done with it:

```bash
kill $VIEWER_PID 2>/dev/null
```

---

## Improving the skill

This is the heart of the loop. You've run the test cases, the user has reviewed the results, and now you need to make the skill better based on their feedback.

### How to think about improvements

1. **Generalize from the feedback.** The big picture thing that's happening here is that we're trying to create skills that can be used a million times (maybe literally, maybe even more who knows) across many different prompts. Here you and the user are iterating on only a few examples over and over again because it helps move faster. The user knows these examples in and out and it's quick for them to assess new outputs. But if the skill you and the user are codeveloping works only for those examples, it's useless. Rather than put in fiddly overfitty changes, or oppressively constrictive MUSTs, if there's some stubborn issue, you might try branching out and using different metaphors, or recommending different patterns of working. It's relatively cheap to try and maybe you'll land on something great.

2. **Keep the prompt lean.** Remove things that aren't pulling their weight. Make sure to read the transcripts, not just the final outputs — if it looks like the skill is making the model waste a bunch of time doing things that are unproductive, you can try getting rid of the parts of the skill that are making it do that and seeing what happens.

3. **Explain the why.** Try hard to explain the **why** behind everything you're asking the model to do. Today's LLMs are *smart*. They have good theory of mind and when given a good harness can go beyond rote instructions and really make things happen. Even if the feedback from the user is terse or frustrated, try to actually understand the task and why the user is writing what they wrote, and what they actually wrote, and then transmit this understanding into the instructions. If you find yourself writing ALWAYS or NEVER in all caps, or using super rigid structures, that's a yellow flag — if possible, reframe and explain the reasoning so that the model understands why the thing you're asking for is important. That's a more humane, powerful, and effective approach.

4. **Look for repeated work across test cases.** Read the transcripts from the test runs and notice if the subagents all independently wrote similar helper scripts or took the same multi-step approach to something. If all 3 test cases resulted in the subagent writing a `create_docx.py` or a `build_chart.py`, that's a strong signal the skill should bundle that script. Write it once, put it in `scripts/`, and tell the skill to use it. This saves every future invocation from reinventing the wheel.

This task is pretty important (we are trying to create billions a year in economic value here!) and your thinking time is not the blocker; take your time and really mull things over. I'd suggest writing a draft revision and then looking at it anew and making improvements. Really do your best to get into the head of the user and understand what they want and need.

### The iteration loop

After improving the skill:

1. Apply your improvements to the skill
2. Rerun all test cases into a new `iteration-<N+1>/` directory, including baseline runs. If you're creating a new skill, the baseline is always `without_skill` (no skill) — that stays the same across iterations. If you're improving an existing skill, use your judgment on what makes sense as the baseline: the original version the user came in with, or the previous iteration.
3. Launch the reviewer with `--previous-workspace` pointing at the previous iteration
4. Wait for the user to review and tell you they're done
5. Read the new feedback, improve again, repeat

Keep going until:
- The user says they're happy
- The feedback is all empty (everything looks good)
- You're not making meaningful progress

---

## Advanced: Blind comparison

For situations where you want a more rigorous comparison between two versions of a skill (e.g., the user asks "is the new version actually better?"), there's a blind comparison system. Read `agents/comparator.md` and `agents/analyzer.md` for the details. The basic idea is: give two outputs to an independent agent without telling it which is which, and let it judge quality. Then analyze why the winner won.

This is optional, requires subagents, and most users won't need it. The human review loop is usually sufficient.

---

## Description Optimization

The description field in SKILL.md frontmatter is the primary mechanism that determines whether Claude invokes a skill. After creating or improving a skill, offer to optimize the description for better triggering accuracy.

### Step 1: Generate trigger eval queries

Create 20 eval queries — a mix of should-trigger and should-not-trigger. Save as JSON:

```json
[
  {"query": "the user prompt", "should_trigger": true},
  {"query": "another prompt", "should_trigger": false}
]
```

The queries must be realistic and something a Claude Code or Claude.ai user would actually type. Not abstract requests, but requests that are concrete and specific and have a good amount of detail. For instance, file paths, personal context about the user's job or situation, column names and values, company names, URLs. A little bit of backstory. Some might be in lowercase or contain abbreviations or typos or casual speech. Use a mix of different lengths, and focus on edge cases rather than making them clear-cut (the user will get a chance to sign off on them).

Bad: `"Format this data"`, `"Extract text from PDF"`, `"Create a chart"`

Good: `"ok so my boss just sent me this xlsx file (its in my downloads, called something like 'Q4 sales final FINAL v2.xlsx') and she wants me to add a column that shows the profit margin as a percentage. The revenue is in column C and costs are in column D i think"`

For the **should-trigger** queries (8-10), think about coverage. You want different phrasings of the same intent — some formal, some casual. Include cases where the user doesn't explicitly name the skill or file type but clearly needs it. Throw in some uncommon use cases and cases where this skill competes with another but should win.

For the **should-not-trigger** queries (8-10), the most valuable ones are the near-misses — queries that share keywords or concepts with the skill but actually need something different. Think adjacent domains, ambiguous phrasing where a naive keyword match would trigger but shouldn't, and cases where the query touches on something the skill does but in a context where another tool is more appropriate.

The key thing to avoid: don't make should-not-trigger queries obviously irrelevant. "Write a fibonacci function" as a negative test for a PDF skill is too easy — it doesn't test anything. The negative cases should be genuinely tricky.

### Step 2: Review with user

Present the eval set to the user for review using the HTML template:

1. Read the template from `assets/eval_review.html`
2. Replace the placeholders:
   - `__EVAL_DATA_PLACEHOLDER__` → the JSON array of eval items (no quotes around it — it's a JS variable assignment)
   - `__SKILL_NAME_PLACEHOLDER__` → the skill's name
   - `__SKILL_DESCRIPTION_PLACEHOLDER__` → the skill's current description
3. Write to a temp file (e.g., `/tmp/eval_review_<skill-name>.html`) and open it: `open /tmp/eval_review_<skill-name>.html`
4. The user can edit queries, toggle should-trigger, add/remove entries, then click "Export Eval Set"
5. The file downloads to `~/Downloads/eval_set.json` — check the Downloads folder for the most recent version in case there are multiple (e.g., `eval_set (1).json`)

This step matters — bad eval queries lead to bad descriptions.

### Step 3: Run the optimization loop

Tell the user: "This will take some time — I'll run the optimization loop in the background and check on it periodically."

Save the eval set to the workspace, then run in the background:

```bash
python -m scripts.run_loop \
  --eval-set <path-to-trigger-eval.json> \
  --skill-path <path-to-skill> \
  --model <model-id-powering-this-session> \
  --max-iterations 5 \
  --verbose
```

Use the model ID from your system prompt (the one powering the current session) so the triggering test matches what the user actually experiences.

While it runs, periodically tail the output to give the user updates on which iteration it's on and what the scores look like.

This handles the full optimization loop automatically. It splits the eval set into 60% train and 40% held-out test, evaluates the current description (running each query 3 times to get a reliable trigger rate), then calls Claude with extended thinking to propose improvements based on what failed. It re-evaluates each new description on both train and test, iterating up to 5 times. When it's done, it opens an HTML report in the browser showing the results per iteration and returns JSON with `best_description` — selected by test score rather than train score to avoid overfitting.

### How skill triggering works

Understanding the triggering mechanism helps design better eval queries. Skills appear in Claude's `available_skills` list with their name + description, and Claude decides whether to consult a skill based on that description. The important thing to know is that Claude only consults skills for tasks it can't easily handle on its own — simple, one-step queries like "read this PDF" may not trigger a skill even if the description matches perfectly, because Claude can handle them directly with basic tools. Complex, multi-step, or specialized queries reliably trigger skills when the description matches.

This means your eval queries should be substantive enough that Claude would actually benefit from consulting a skill. Simple queries like "read file X" are poor test cases — they won't trigger skills regardless of description quality.

### Step 4: Apply the result

Take `best_description` from the JSON output and update the skill's SKILL.md frontmatter. Show the user before/after and report the scores.

---

### Package and Present (only if `present_files` tool is available)

Check whether you have access to the `present_files` tool. If you don't, skip this step. If you do, package the skill and present the .skill file to the user:

```bash
python -m scripts.package_skill <path/to/skill-folder>
```

After packaging, direct the user to the resulting `.skill` file path so they can install it.

## Claude.ai-specific instructions

In Claude.ai, the core workflow is the same (draft → test → review → improve → repeat), but because Claude.ai doesn't have subagents, some mechanics change. Here's what to adapt:

**Running test cases**: No subagents means no parallel execution. For each test case, read the skill's SKILL.md, then follow its instructions to accomplish the test prompt yourself. Do them one at a time. This is less rigorous than independent subagents (you wrote the skill and you're also running it, so you have full context), but it's a useful sanity check — and the human review step compensates. Skip the baseline runs — just use the skill to complete the task as requested.

**Reviewing results**: If you can't open a browser (e.g., Claude.ai's VM has no display, or you're on a remote server), skip the browser reviewer entirely. Instead, present results directly in the conversation. For each test case, show the prompt and the output. If the output is a file the user needs to see (like a .docx or .xlsx), save it to the filesystem and tell them where it is so they can download and inspect it. Ask for feedback inline: "How does this look? Anything you'd change?"

**Benchmarking**: Skip the quantitative benchmarking — it relies on baseline comparisons which aren't meaningful without subagents. Focus on qualitative feedback from the user.

**The iteration loop**: Same as before — improve the skill, rerun the test cases, ask for feedback — just without the browser reviewer in the middle. You can still organize results into iteration directories on the filesystem if you have one.

**Description optimization**: This section requires the `claude` CLI tool (specifically `claude -p`) which is only available in Claude Code. Skip it if you're on Claude.ai.

**Blind comparison**: Requires subagents. Skip it.

**Packaging**: The `package_skill.py` script works anywhere with Python and a filesystem. On Claude.ai, you can run it and the user can download the resulting `.skill` file.

---

## Cowork-Specific Instructions

If you're in Cowork, the main things to know are:

- You have subagents, so the main workflow (spawn test cases in parallel, run baselines, grade, etc.) all works. (However, if you run into severe problems with timeouts, it's OK to run the test prompts in series rather than parallel.)
- You don't have a browser or display, so when generating the eval viewer, use `--static <output_path>` to write a standalone HTML file instead of starting a server. Then proffer a link that the user can click to open the HTML in their browser.
- For whatever reason, the Cowork setup seems to disincline Claude from generating the eval viewer after running the tests, so just to reiterate: whether you're in Cowork or in Claude Code, after running tests, you should always generate the eval viewer for the human to look at examples before revising the skill yourself and trying to make corrections, using `generate_review.py` (not writing your own boutique html code). Sorry in advance but I'm gonna go all caps here: GENERATE THE EVAL VIEWER *BEFORE* evaluating inputs yourself. You want to get them in front of the human ASAP!
- Feedback works differently: since there's no running server, the viewer's "Submit All Reviews" button will download `feedback.json` as a file. You can then read it from there (you may have to request access first).
- Packaging works — `package_skill.py` just needs Python and a filesystem.
- Description optimization (`run_loop.py` / `run_eval.py`) should work in Cowork just fine since it uses `claude -p` via subprocess, not a browser, but please save it until you've fully finished making the skill and the user agrees it's in good shape.

## Reference files

The agents/ directory contains instructions for specialized subagents. Read them when you need to spawn the relevant subagent.

- `agents/grader.md` — How to evaluate assertions against outputs
- `agents/comparator.md` — How to do blind A/B comparison between two outputs
- `agents/analyzer.md` — How to analyze why one version beat another

The references/ directory has additional documentation:
- `references/schemas.md` — JSON structures for evals.json, grading.json, etc.

---

Repeating one more time the core loop here for emphasis:

- Figure out what the skill is about
- Draft or edit the skill
- Run claude-with-access-to-the-skill on test prompts
- With the user, evaluate the outputs:
  - Create benchmark.json and run `eval-viewer/generate_review.py` to help the user review them
  - Run quantitative evals
- Repeat until you and the user are satisfied
- Package the final skill and return it to the user.

Please add steps to your TodoList, if you have such a thing, to make sure you don't forget. If you're in Cowork, please specifically put "Create evals JSON and run `eval-viewer/generate_review.py` so human can review test cases" in your TodoList to make sure it happens.

Good luck!
